perturbation.original_text: In any action or proceeding, whether or not re sulting in litigation, between Buyer (or either of them) and Seller, or between Buyer (or either of them) and Broker, including any litigation to enforce any of the terms of this agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover, in addition to any damages or compensation received, its costs and expenses incurred in connection with such action or proceeding, including any reasonable attorneys ’ fees, expenses and court costs.
perturbation.changed_text: In any action or proceeding, whether or not re sulting in litigation, between Buyer (or either of them) and Seller, or between Buyer (or either of them) and Broker, including any litigation to enforce any of the terms of this agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover its costs and expenses incurred in connection with such action or proceeding. The detail including any reasonable attorneys’ fees, expenses and court costs will be subjected to further mutual agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that the prevailing party is entitled to recover costs, expenses, and attorney's fees. The modified text adds that the recovery of attorney's fees is subject to further mutual agreement, which reduces the enforceability of the prevailing party's right to recover all expenses, creating ambiguity and weakening the original clause. This could be interpreted as a waiver or limitation of rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1033.5(a)(10) states that attorney's fees, when authorized by contract, are allowable as costs. By making the recovery of attorney's fees subject to further mutual agreement, the modified text contradicts the established right to recover attorney's fees as part of costs under California law when a contract provides for it.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1033.5. (a) The following items are allowable as costs under Section 1032: (1) Filing, motion, and jury fees. (2) Juror food and lodging while they are kept together during trial and after the jury retires for deliberation. (3) (A) Taking, video recording, and transcribing necessary depositions, including an original and one copy of those taken by the claimant and one copy of depositions taken by the party against whom costs are allowed. (B) Fees of a certified or registered interpreter for the deposition of a party or witness who does not proficiently speak or understand the English language. (C) Travel expenses to attend depositions. (4) Service of process by a public officer, registered process server, or other means, as follows: (A) When service is by a public officer, the recoverable cost is the fee authorized by law at the time of service. (B) If service is by a process server registered pursuant to Chapter 16 (commencing with Section 22350) of Division 8 of the Business and Professions Code, the recoverable cost is the amount actually i
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction. The original text mandates the prevailing party's entitlement to recover attorney's fees. The changed text makes the recovery of attorney's fees contingent upon further mutual agreement. This weakens the prevailing party's right. CCP 1033.5(a) states that attorney's fees are allowable as costs under Section 1032. By subjecting attorney's fees to 'further mutual agreement,' the modified text undermines the established right to recover these fees as part of costs under California law, when the contract initially provides for it. This undermines the legal obligation outlined in CCP 1033.5(a).

perturbation.original_text: Buyer acknowledge s and agree s that any breach of any of its/his/her obligations hereunder will cause Seller and the Business irreparable harm for which Seller and the Business have no adequate remedy at law, and that Seller and the Business shall be entitled to injunctive and other equitable relief to prevent a breach or continued breach of this agreement, in addition to any other remedies Seller and Business may have at law or in equity, and that this agreement shall be specifically enforceable in accordance with its terms.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer acknowledge s and agree s that any breach of any of its/his/her obligations hereunder will cause Seller and the Business irreparable harm. Seller and the Business shall be entitled to injunctive relief to prevent a breach or continued breach of this agreement, in addition to any other remedies Seller and Business may have at law or in equity, and that this agreement shall be specifically enforceable in accordance with its terms. For all other remedies, Buyer can appeal to the local judicial system.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'For all other remedies, Buyer can appeal to the local judicial system', it introduces ambiguity about the process. It contradicts the previous statement that the agreement is specifically enforceable and that injunctive relief is available. This may give the impression that normal judicial procedures can replace those prescribed remedies.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3422 outlines when injunctive relief is appropriate. The added phrase suggests alternative legal pathways outside of the specific enforcement terms agreed upon, potentially undermining the enforceability of the injunctive relief clause and the overall agreement's specific performance provisions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3422 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. SPECIFIC AND PREVENTIVE RELIEF [3366 - 3424] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Preventive Relief [3420 - 3424] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 3422. Except where otherwise provided by this Title, a final injunction may be granted to prevent the breach of an obligation existing in favor of the applicant: 1. Where pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief; 2. Where it would be extremely difficult to ascertain the amount of compensation which would afford adequate relief; 3. Where the restraint is necessary to prevent a multiplicity of judicial proceedings; or, 4. Where the obligation arises from a trust. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The added sentence, "For all other remedies, Buyer can appeal to the local judicial system," introduces ambiguity regarding the available remedies and enforcement mechanisms. This contradicts the initial statement that the agreement is specifically enforceable and that injunctive relief is available. California Civil Code § 3422, as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1, outlines the conditions under which injunctive relief may be granted. The added phrase undermines the enforceability of the injunctive relief clause and the agreement's specific performance provisions because it suggests alternative pathways that bypass the agreed-upon remedies, creating a conflict with the original intent of the contract as it relates to injunctive relief.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall be governed by and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of California, USA as applicable to contracts to be performed therein .
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall be loosely governed by and enforced in accordance with general contract law of the United States.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to the 'general contract law of the United States' removes the specificity of California law, making it more difficult to determine the precise applicable laws and regulations. Using 'loosely governed' introduces further ambiguity about the enforcement standards.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1646 states that a contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed, or, if that place is not indicated, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. By removing the reference to California law and replacing it with a vague reference to 'general contract law of the United States,' the contract becomes less clear about which state's laws apply, potentially conflicting with this principle.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1646 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1646. A contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed; or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts California Civil Code § 1646, as explained by the LLM and supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states that a contract should be interpreted according to the law of the place where it is to be performed. The original text specifies California law, while the changed text uses 'general contract law of the United States,' removing this specificity and creating ambiguity, thereby contradicting the requirement for clarity in applicable laws as described in CIV 1646.
